mnemonics.title: V-1 Iterations Rule
mnemonics.content: V-1 ITERATIONS: Think 'V'ertices minus ONE makes PERFECT paths. A simple path can't have cycles, so V-1 edges MAX!

mnemonics.title: Negative Cycle Detection
mnemonics.content: DETECT NEGATIVE: Do THE Extra (V-th) Check. If Edge Still Relaxes, Negative Cycle!

mnemonics.title: Initialization
mnemonics.content: S.O.S. = Source is Zero, Others are S (∞ - Infinity/Sky high)

mnemonics.title: Bellman-Ford vs Dijkstra
mnemonics.content: BIG BELLMAN: Both Find Paths, But Bellman Handles Bad (negative) weights, Dijkstra Doesn't. B = Bad weights, D = Doesn't do negatives

acronyms.usage: Time complexity of Bellman-Ford: O(V × E)
Number of iterations in Bellman-Ford algorithm
Tree formed by predecessor links showing shortest paths from source

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a rubber band (path) between two points. If you find a shorter route through an intermediate point, you 'relax' or tighten the rubber band to the shorter distance.
Think of building a path step by step. First iteration finds 1-hop paths, second finds 2-hop paths, and so on. After V-1 steps, you've covered all possible simple paths.

Imagine walking on a path where each step gives you money (negative weight). You could walk forever and become infinitely rich. The V-th iteration tries to walk one more time - if you still gain money, you've found this infinite profit cycle!
Think of distance array as a distance board at a train station. Source platform is 0 (you're already there). All other platforms show 'NOT CONNECTED' (infinity) until you find a route to them.

analogies.analogy: Like learning to find the shortest route to each location in a city. First you learn all 1-way routes, then 2-way routes, then 3-way... After V-1 rounds, you know all possible direct and indirect routes.
Like GPS finding a shorter route. If going through an intermediate point (like a gas station on the way) saves time/money, you update your route - that's relaxation!
Think of edge weights as costs (positive) or discounts (negative). A negative weight edge is like a coupon that reduces your total cost. Bellman-Ford accounts for these discounts properly.

A negative cycle is like a promotional loop where you pay to enter but get more money back than you paid - you can go around infinitely and become arbitrarily rich! This makes 'shortest path' meaningless.
